coredata.dc:title: A selective multi-emission chemiluminescence system using hollow Eu3+/Gd2O3@SiO2 spheres modified by naphthalene derived molecules for the quantification of Cr6+ ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nouri, Maryam
Hajiaghababaei, Leila
Badiei, Alireza
Khalilian, Faezeh
Mazloomifar, Ali
coredata.dc:description: We developed a multi-emission fluorescence sensor based on hybridizing two different fluorescent centers, which provides a built-in correction to remove environmental effects. Eu3+ doped Gd2O3 nanoparticles were embedded in the silica hollow spheres while the fluorophore naphthalene derived molecules were covalently linked to the surface of silica to form multi-emission fluorescence sensors (Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO). With a detection limit of 3.8 nM, the nanosensor offers an effective platform for reliable Cr6+ detection. The obtained accuracy is considerably lower than the maximum level of Cr6+ in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). The prepared Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO inherited simultaneously the excellent luminescence performance of Eu3+/Gd2O3 and the fluorophore group and exhibited interesting structural and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution. A higher enhancement of fluorescence emission stemming from an intrinsic structure of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles was observed by adding Cr6+ ions as opposed to naphthalene molecules. There was a good linear relationship between the sum of fluorescence intensity changes (ΔI330 + ΔI610) and the Cr6+ concentration in the range of 0.1–1.0 ppm. The nanosensor fabricated by this method showed good reversibility, enabling the rapid detection of Cr6+ in real water samples. As a result of this groundbreaking study, we are able to develop an idea for building a multifunctional fluorescent probe, with potential applications in biotechnology, food analysis, and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hollow spheres
Gd2O3 nanoparticles
Fluorescent sensor
Cr6+ detection
Eu3+ doped

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of hollow core–shell Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 structure
Synthesis of hollow core–shell Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO structure

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Islamic Azad University of Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Islamic Azad University of Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) ShahreRey branch and the University of Tehran for assisting this research financially.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Illustration of the morphological evolution of the prepared nanosensors and corresponding FESEM (a-e) and TEM (f and g) images of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO fabrication procedure.

(a (i-v)) Powder XRD patterns of the Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 samples with varying concentrations of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles, ranging from samples without nanoparticles to those with the highest concentration (from (i) to (v)); (b) Particle size distribution in aqueous solution of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of HSiO2 (i), Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 (ii) and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO (iii); (d) FTIR spectrum of HSiO2 (i), Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 (ii) and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO (iii); (e) Thermogravimetric analysis of HSiO2 (i), Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 (ii) and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO (iii).

(a) Emission spectra of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 (i) and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO (ii) in the aqueous solution; (b) Emission spectra of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO treated with different metal ions; (c) Fluorescence spectrum of the Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO at different concentrations of Cr6+ (0.1–1.0 ppm); (d) Linear relationship between the sum of fluorescence intensity changes and the Cr6+ concentration; (e,f) Linear fitting curve of Cr6+ concentration (0.1–1.0 ppm) vs (e) ΔI330 and (f) ΔI610.
(a) Comparison of the luminescence intensity of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO exposed to mixed cations; (b) Sum of the fluorescence intensity changes as a function of different pH value.

head.ce:title: A selective multi-emission chemiluminescence system using hollow Eu3+/Gd2O3@SiO2 spheres modified by naphthalene derived molecules for the quantification of Cr6+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed a multi-emission fluorescence sensor based on hybridizing two different fluorescent centers, which provides a built-in correction to remove environmental effects. Eu3+ doped Gd2O3 nanoparticles were embedded in the silica hollow spheres while the fluorophore naphthalene derived molecules were covalently linked to the surface of silica to form multi-emission fluorescence sensors (Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO). With a detection limit of 3.8 nM, the nanosensor offers an effective platform for reliable Cr6+ detection. The obtained accuracy is considerably lower than the maximum level of Cr6+ in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). The prepared Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO inherited simultaneously the excellent luminescence performance of Eu3+/Gd2O3 and the fluorophore group and exhibited interesting structural and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution. A higher enhancement of fluorescence emission stemming from an intrinsic structure of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles was observed by adding Cr6+ ions as opposed to naphthalene molecules. There was a good linear relationship between the sum of fluorescence intensity changes (ΔI330 + ΔI610) and the Cr6+ concentration in the range of 0.1–1.0 ppm. The nanosensor fabricated by this method showed good reversibility, enabling the rapid detection of Cr6+ in real water samples. As a result of this groundbreaking study, we are able to develop an idea for building a multifunctional fluorescent probe, with potential applications in biotechnology, food analysis, and environmental analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and selective detection of heavy metal ions is of considerable current interest in chemistry owing to their significant importance in biology, and environmental assays [1–5]. Water pollution and health risks could arise from chromium ions in particular. These ions exist in two main oxidation states in aqueous media: Cr3+ and Cr6+ [6–8]. Cr(III) has an important role in carbohydrate metabolism, enzyme activation and nucleic acid stabilization [9,10]. However, Cr(VI) is a well-known toxic carcinogen which is released into the environment mainly via industrial routes like chrome plating, pigment production, battery manufacturing, leather tanning, dying, sanitary landfill leaching, and stainless processing [11,12]. There is also epidemiological evidence that Cr6+ compounds can cause lung cancer and DNA point mutations [13,14]. There is therefore a need for the faster, facile, and quantitative detection of trace aqueous Cr(VI) from the viewpoint of environmental monitoring and protection [15]. Various spectroscopic techniques have been developed in recent years for the detection of hexavalent chromium, including chemiluminescence, X-ray fluorescence, atomic absorption spectroscopy, chromatography, stripping voltammetry, electrochemical, and amperometric [11,16–19]. However, these methods are expensive and have high equipment requirements. On the other hand, selective detection of Cr6+ ions is difficult in aqueous solution because Cr6+ is easily reduced to Cr3+ during sample pretreatment [20]. Hence, a selective assay method for Cr6+ ions, irrespective of the presence of Cr3+, is urgently required.

Nowadays, the fluorescence sensor is developed due to the superior sensitivity, its low detection limit spatial and temporal resolution, quality and relatively low operating costs [21–24]. Most fluorescent probe analyses are based on single signal changes, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancement [25,26]. In comparison, the preparation of multi-emission fluorescence probes appears to be a new area of research, and this is owing to the wide range of potential applications in analyte recognition and since it provides a measurable and divergent output change in the optical signal [27–30]. In order to prevent interference, it is possible to prepare fluorescent probes with different fluorescent signal outputs, which can be used in fluorescence detection to weaken the influence of changes in excitation light, environment, and probe concentration [31–33].

Many fluorescent probes based on carbon quantum dots, rhodamine, coumarin, and other fluorophores have been explored for metal ion detection [34–37]. The unstable nature and photo-bleaching ability with the broad emission behavior of organic fluorescent sensors has reduced their sensitivity. Lanthanide-based nanoparticles are well known for their high photostability, narrow emission, and amendable quantum yield, making them excellent sensing platforms. Especially Gd2O3 NPs have been widely used as an effective contrast agent in MRI and have also been useful in the detection of metal ions and pesticides [38,39]. However, nanoparticles have a number of limitations, such as particle growth, photo-induced decomposition, conjugate aggregation, and rapid photo-bleaching that limit the application of these fluorescent nanomaterials. A second approach is to disperse fluorescent nanoparticles in organic matrices which isolate the nanoparticles from external perturbations, improving their physicochemical stability. [40,41]. Mesoporous silica hollow spheres have emerged as one of the most robust matrix for sensing applications owing to the distinctive characteristics including tailored mesoporous structure, high specific surface area, large pore volume, superior biocompatibility, facile surface functionalization and optical transparency [42–44]. More interestingly, after modification of SiO2 with fluorescent nanoparticles, covalent immobilization of a fluorophore in a silica matrix can be used for generating a multi-emission fluorescent probe with high emission efficiency.

Based on the above considerations, this study develops a multi-emission fluorescent sensor for detecting Cr6+ ions using organic fluorescence molecules and Eu3+ doped Gd2O3 nanoparticles. For this purpose, an innovative hollow structure consisting of naphthalene derived molecules grafted on hollow silica spheres and decorated with Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles was prepared using a templating method and subsequent solvothermal process. The designed probe shows multi-emission wavelengths under single excitation, and it can detect metal ions based on the fluorescence intensity changes of two independent emission peaks.
All used materials and instrumentation data are available in Supplementary Information.

Silica spheres with a hollow structure were designed by using the hard template method, which is displayed in Fig. 1 . At the beginning, the uniform polystyrene (PS) nanospheres were synthesized as template by a feasibly polymerization of the styrene monomer in the aqueous solution via 2,2′-azobis-(2-methylpropionamide) dihydrochloride as an initiator. To better coordinate between the silica precursor and the PS template, the PS was first pretreated with poly (allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH). The extra procedure was based on the steps stated in refs [45]. Accordingly, the prepared PS spheres were dispersed in distilled water (concentration of 60 g/L). A 15 mL mixture of PAH in water and sodium chloride was prepared (c = 1.6 g/L in NaCl and c = 1.5 g/L in PAH). 4 mL of the prepared PS template colloidal dispersion was added to the above mixture dropwise under stirring. After stirring for 1 h, the resulting dispersion was centrifuged, washed twice with distilled water, and redispersed in a solution of PVP in ethanol (c = 6 g/L). After another 1 h of stirring, the mixture was centrifuged and redispersed in 20 mL of fresh ethanol. To form the SiO2 shell around the PS cores, ammonia CTAB (0.1 g), (1.8 mL) and TEOS (2.2 mL) were added subsequently to the above solution. The resulting mixture was stirred for 4 h at 35 °C, centrifuged, washed three times with deionized water and ethanol repeatedly and dried at 75 °C for 8 h. The polystyrene in the core was eliminated by heating the core–shell particles at 550 ˚C under air atmosphere, leaving hollow SiO2 particles [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hollow core–shell Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 structure
ce:section.ce:para: Decoration of europium-doped Gd2O3 nanoparticles on silica hollow spherical substrates was performed by homogeneous precipitation from europium nitrate, gadolinium nitrate, and urea aqueous solutions. The detailed procedure used for the synthesis was as follows: first, gadolinium oxide and europium oxide were dissolved in a diluted nitric acid solution (1:1) and evaporated to dryness, then dissolved in deionized water to form homogeneous gadolinium nitrate and europium nitrate solutions. Second, 50 mmol of fabricated SiO2 hollow nanospheres was ultrasonically added to the stock solutions containing 0.04 mol L−1 Gd(NO3)3, 0.008 mol L−1 Eu(NO3)3, and 1.5 mol L−1 (NH2)2CO. The obtained mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 50 ml and heated at 100 °C for 8 h. Next, the temperature of the autoclave was naturally reduced to room temperature and the resulting product was filtered and washed with ethanol four times. Finally, the prepared samples were heated at 800 °C in air for 3 h after drying at 70 °C for 6 h. Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles were produced under identical conditions, excluding the HSiO2 substrate. Additionally, identical conditions were applied to create Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2, using varying concentrations of gadolinium and europium salts to produce different amounts of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hollow core–shell Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO structure
ce:section.ce:para: For this purpose, 4 mmol 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane and 4 mmol 1-amino-naphthalene was added to 60 ml dry toluene and refluxed for 8 h under N2 atmosphere. After that, 1.5 g Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 spheres were added to the solution and the mixture was refluxed for another 36 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Then, the solid product was filtered and washed with excess ethanol. At the end, the obtained pale pink solid was dried overnight and marked as Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO.

To conduct the fluorescence tests, 0.005 g of nanohybrid sensor were dispersed in 100 ml of Millipore water using an ultrasonic instrument. Stock solutions of metal ions were prepared using appropriate nitrate salts. For the stock solution of chromium(VI), K2Cr2O7 was utilized. 2 mL of the prepared sensor material and 50 µL of different metal ions were employed to form the fluorescence spectrum. For the competition tests, besides the selected ion, other metal cations as interfering agents with a ratio of 1:5 were added to the 2 mL of the prepared solution. Additionally, for the titration tests, various amounts of the metal cation were added to the 2 mL of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO dispersion. The fluorescence experiments were conducted with 280 nm excitation wavelength.

The overall construction procedure for the synthesis of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO and the corresponding FESEM and TEM images of the nanospheres at each preparation step are presented in Fig. 1. The fabrication procedure consists of five major steps: (i) synthesis of the monodispersed spherical PS as the starting templates; (ii) coating of the PS cores with a uniform shell of SiO2 to obtain core–shell structure; (iii) transformation of spherical nanoparticles to hollow silica spheres via selective removal of PS cores; (iv) the decoration of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles on the surface of HSiO2 via hydrothermal process; (v) finally grafting of the fluorophore group on the surface of the silica substrate to achieve final structure. Fig. 1(a–e) indicates FESEM images at each step of the fabrication process. Fig. 1a clearly shows that as-obtained PS templates have a uniform structure with spherical morphology and an average diameter of 250 nm. After the polycondensation of silica on the polystyrene surface, core–shell particles were obtained, which had a monodispersed spherical nanostructure with an average diameter of 260 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c and 1f accordingly show the FESEM and TEM images of HSiO2 after removing the PS template, respectively. The hollow interior of HSiO2 is confirmed by the TEM image and the size of the core cavity was estimated at 250 nm, which is like the size of PS nanosphere templates. Furthermore, from the FESEM image (Fig. 1c) the hollow space within HSiO2 is easily observable in certain perforated HSiO2 formations, indicated by yellow arrows. The FESEM and TEM images of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 are shown in Fig. 1d and 1 g, respectively. After hydrothermal decoration of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles on the surface of HSiO2, it can be seen that Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 structures largely retain the spherical morphology of HSiO2. However, Eu3+/Gd2O3 were not clearly visible in the FESEM image, because of their tiny size and low content. From TEM measurements (Fig. 1f), The HSiO2 is intimately decorated by a few Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles, which exhibit a unique core–shell structure. Furthermore, it is obvious from Fig. 1e that structure maintains spherical morphology after being grafted by a fluorophore group.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted to characterize the resulting nanoparticles (Fig. 2 a). Hollow silica spheres exhibited no apparent reflections except for a broad band centered at 24°, proving an amorphous structure (characteristic reflection for amorphous SiO2, JCPDS 29-0085) [47]. It is worth noting that the characteristic diffractions corresponding to Eu3+/Gd2O3 phase can be hardly detected in Eu3+/Gd2O3@SiO2, probably owing to the low content of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles in the composite sample. Additionally, these observations further reveal that the reflection intensity of Gd2O3 phase will increase with increasing content of Eu3+/Gd2O3, proving a good dispersion of these nanoparticles on the sensor surface. For composites with high content of Eu3+/Gd2O3, in addition to that early reflection related to SiO2, four new characteristic reflections appear, which can be well-matched with the standard diffraction data for pure Gd2O3 nanoparticles without other impurity phases (JCPDS card No. 12-0797)[48]. Furthermore, Fig. S1(a) displays the presence of Si2p (101.5 eV) on the Eu3+/Gd2O3@SiO2 particles. When compared to the pure silica spectra (103.1 eV), there is a chemical shift of 1.6 eV. Moreover, Fig. S1(b) and (c) indicate a chemical shift of 0.7 eV for Gd3d and 0.5 eV for O1s, respectively, after the Eu/Gd2O3 coating on the silica particles. These shifts are caused by the chemical environment change resulting from the close interaction between Gd2O3 and Silica substrate. This again demonstrates the interaction among species in the binary sensor. If silica particles were the source of the SiO2 peaks, the binding energy of Si2p should match that of the standard silica spectra. However, in our findings, the binding energy of Si2p has a chemical shift of 1.5 eV compared to the standard silica spectra.

Fig. 2b exhibits the particle size distribution of the hybrid sensor, which was performed using the static light scattering (SLS) method. According to the distribution, the average size of sensor nanoparticles is about 250 nm, which is consistent with the electron microscopy results.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of HSiO2, Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO are displayed in Fig. 2c. The type-IV isotherm typical of mesoporous materials are shown in all three hollow samples. The results of the surface area determined by the BET model, the pore volume, and pore size by the BJH method are presented in Table S1. The surface area of pure HSiO2 (672 m2 g−1) is more than the surface area of its functionalized compounds, i.e., Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 (481 m2 g−1) and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO (285 m2 g−1). This result can be ascribed to the blockage of HSiO2 pores by Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles incorporated on the surface of HSiO2 and the subsequent modification with a fluorophore group, which led to a reduction in accessible surface area for gas adsorption. Nevertheless, the Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO composites still possess a high surface area and large pore volume which are beneficial for sensing applications.

In Fig. 2d for the as-formed hollow SiO2 spheres, the absorption bands owing to OH (3435 cm−1), H2O (1637 cm−1), Si-O-Si (asymmetric stretching, 1100 cm−1; symmetric stretching, 803 cm−1), Si-OH (symmetric stretching, 949 cm−1), and Si-O (bending, 469 cm−1) are seen. This exhibits that the as formed SiO2 spheres contain a large amount of OH groups and H2O in their shell. The surface Si-OH groups play a vital role in bonding the metal ions (Eu3+, Gd3+) for decoration of the Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles on the hollow SiO2 shell in the following annealing step. For pure Eu3+/Gd2O3 sample and core–shell Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 sample, the weak peak centered at 545 cm−1 corresponds to the absorption of Gd (Eu)O bonds, which further prove the formation of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles on the surfaces of the hollow silica spheres. Furthermore, the peak related to OH groups from the as formed SiO2 nanoparticles has almost disappeared for Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 sample heated at 800 °C, which exhibits the removal of adsorbed water and the complete dehydration of free silanol group [49]. A series of bands of 2870–2970 cm−1 were generated by stretching vibrations of methylene −(–CH2-)- in propyl chains following grafting of fluorophore group. The bands around 1650 and 1565 cm−1 corresponded to the CO stretching vibrations and the bending vibrations of –NH groups, proving the formation of amide groups. The weak bands around 1400 and 1445 cm−1 were attributed to CN and ring CC stretching vibrations, respectively. Therefore, these results indicate successful covalent grafting of organic moieties onto the hollow silica surface.

Thermal behaviors of the pure hollow sphere sample and functionalized with fluorophore groups and nanoparticles were investigated using thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA). As shown in Fig. 2e, the first loss of 3 mass % is observed both for pure and modified hollow spheres, and can be ascribed to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water. With the increase in temperature from 150 to 400 °C, thermal-induced decomposition of fluorophore groups leads to a further decrease in the mass of modified hollow silica particles (16 %). Such a sharp weight loss was not detected in the case of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 sample. From these data, it is evident that the prepared Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 is stable up to 800 °C. However, a slight decline in mass percentage can be detected in the pure hollow silica and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 samples, which is attributed to the dehydration condensation of the surface hydroxyl group. The TGA analysis suggests that around 13 % of the fluorophore has been graphed on the hollow sphere surface.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of both the Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2 and Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO samples was examined using an excitation wavelength of 280 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. The detected transitions in the emission spectra are consistent with the characteristic transitions of Eu3+, indicating the successful integration of Eu3+ ions into the Gd2O3 nanoparticles [50]. The PL spectrum of both samples shows peaks that correspond to the 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 1, 2, 3, 4) transition lines of Eu3+ [51]. The crystalline Gd2O3 belongs to the cubic system with space group Ia3. This type of structure contains two crystallographically different cation sites with C2 (non-centrosymmetric) and S6 (centrosymmetric) respectively, and the ratio of C2 to S6 sites is 3:1 (i.e., in Gd2O3 75 % Gd3+ ions occupy the C2 sites, 25 % Gd3+ occupy the S6 sites) [52]. The doped rare earth ions, such as Eu3+, are assumed to occupy these two types of sites in a statistical way. The electric-dipole transition is only permitted when the Eu3+ ions are situated within the crystalline lattice in sites lacking a center of symmetry. Eu3+ ions located at the C2 sites have a concentration three times higher than those at the S6 sites, resulting in the generation of very strong electric dipole 5D0 → 7F2 forced transitions [53]. The 5D0→ 7F0 transition of Eu3+, which is strongly forbidden for S6 site, is also from Eu3+ (C2). This transition is forbidden by the electric dipole selection rules because both the 5D0 and 7F0 states have the same parity, making it very weak and not observable in the spectrum. The emission peaks spanning from 645 nm to 670 nm and the range from 681 nm to 719 nm predominantly correspond to transitions from 5D0 to 7F1 and 5D0 to 7F3, respectively, associated with Eu3+ ions [54]. These emissions arise from the magnetic dipole (MD) allowed transitions, and their intensity remains independent of the chemical surroundings of the active ions [55]. At the same excitation wavelength, in addition to PL emissions of Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles, Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO shows a strong naphthalene-based emission at 330 nm. It appears that the designed nanosensors here indicate intense absorption in the ultraviolet region which is converted to powerful emissions in the red region. These results all prove that hollow structured multi-emission fluorescence probes are successfully prepared.

Considering the excellent fluorescence properties of such as strong multi-fluorescence emission, interesting structure with high surface area and high fluorescence stability, we explored their potential sensing abilities toward metal cations at room temperature. As we discussed earlier, under the excitation of 280 nm, the emission spectrum of consisted of two parts: the emission wavelength around 330 nm owing to the fluorophore group and the other emission wavelength located between 550 nm and 750 nm due to Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles. Engineered nanosensor was immersed in aqueous solutions containing 1mM (Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Co2+ and Cr6+) to form metal-ion-loaded nanohybrid sensor suspensions. The corresponding photoluminescence properties still exhibited the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+/Gd2O3 and naphthalene derived molecules. However, only the highest emission peak related to Eu3+/Gd2O3 centered at 610 nm was considered for a more accurate comparison.

Obviously, the fluorescence intensities of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO were deeply dependent on the type of metal ion and only Cr6+ ions showed an excellent enhancement effect (Fig. 3b). The relative intensity of emission peaks enhanced to 3.95 and 2.44 for Eu3+/Gd2O3 part and NCO part, respectively. The significant luminescence enhancement effect of Cr6+ ions indicated that nanohybrid was promising luminescent sensors for Cr6+.

To better understand the luminescence responses of Cr6+ ions, we examined photoluminescence titration using different concentrations of Cr6+ ions. As shown in Fig. 3c, the luminescence intensity was enhanced gradually with increasing Cr6+ concentration from 0.1 to 1.0 ppm. Initially, the fluorescence intensity of naphthalene at 330 nm was higher than that of Eu3+/Gd2O3 at 610 nm. On the contrary, when the concentration of Cr6+ reached to 1 ppm, the fluorescence intensity at 610 nm was slightly stronger than that at 330 nm, proving that the fluorescence intensity increase rate of E3+/Gd2O3 was higher than that of NCO group.

Fig. 3d discloses a good linear relationship between Cr6+ concentration and the sum of fluorescence intensity changes (ΔI330 + ΔI610, ΔI330 = I'330-I330, ΔI610 = I'610-I610, I330 and I610 are the fluorescence intensities at 330 nm and 610 nm, respectively). The linear equation was Y = 405.58 X + 131.33 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9849. The linear detection range was from 0.1 ppm to 1.0 ppm. It can be clearly seen that the multifunctional fluorescent sensor performs better than the single one (Fig. 3e and f).

Further investigate the sensitivity and selectivity of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO towards Cr6+ ion relative to other competitive ions, we performed the interference test. The gained results reveal that the ratiometric emission enhancement caused by the addition of Cr6+ with other competitive species (5-fold in concentration) was corresponding to that caused by Cr6+ alone (Fig. 4 a), and these consequences were clearly proved the sensor Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO can be utilized for the ratiometric detection of Cr6+ ion selectively even in the presence of other competitive metal ions. Taken together, all the above experimental results confirm that Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO can selectively recognize Cr6+ in the presence of two or more metal cations. Additionally, the value of pH is usually considered as a significant factor in fluorescence response. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the sum of the fluorescence intensity changes increases with solution pH varying from 5.0 to 7.0 and then decreases with further pH increasing. Therefore, pH 7 is chosen was chosen as the optimum pH condition which is conceivable for the environmental and biological applications.

Recyclability is a remarkably significant parameter to evaluate the performance of the fluorescence sensor in practical application. The used samples are simply collected from the solution by fast centrifugalizing and washed many times in distilled water and ultimately dried at 60 °C for the next cycle. These samples can be easily re-dispersed well in the reaction media for subsequent continuous experiments. It is clear that the luminescent sensing property of the reused sample exhibits no obvious change after five successive runs (Fig. S2), exhibiting that the as-prepared nanohybrid sensor possesses good stability and reusability as a luminescent sensor toward Cr6+.

To validate the practical application of Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO, the developed assay was successfully used for the quantitative determination of Cr6+ ions in tap water and industrial wastewater. At first, the collected water samples were filtered through Whatman filter paper to eliminate impurities and suspended matter, after that it was boiled for 3 min to remove dissolved gases. Then, samples are spiked with standard Cr6+ ions at two different concentration levels and then diluted into working linear range and analyzed directly with the proposed standard addition assay. For comparison, the Cr6+ content in real samples was measured using the ICP-OES technique. As displayed in Table S2, the analytical results of the developed method were the same as those calculated by ICP-OES method. The results obtained by the developed method are in good agreement with an average spiked sample recovery as shown in Table S2. Therefore, Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO can be applicable to Cr6+ determinations in different environmental water samples.

In conclusion, by preparing a mixture of multi-emission silica embedded Eu3+/Gd2O3, in conjugation with naphthalene derived molecules, we have designed a selective and sensitive strategy for the multi-emission fluorescent detection of Cr6+. The fabricated nanohybrid sensor exhibited dual distinct fluorescent signals at 610 nm and 330 nm, which originated from Eu3+/Gd2O3 nanoparticles and fluorophore group, respectively. The enhancement in fluorescent intensity of the nanohybrid sensor at 610 nm was larger than that enhancement at 330 nm, exhibiting the fluorescence intensity increase rate of Eu3+/Gd2O3 was higher than that of naphthalene-based molecules. Moreover, the prepared sensor indicates extremely high sensitivity for Cr(VI) and excellent selectivity response for Cr6+ over other heavy-metal ions at the millimolar level. The Eu3+/Gd2O3@HSiO2/NCO hybrid sphere has provided an effective platform for rapid and reliable detection of Cr6+ in a concentration range from 0.1 ppm to 1.0 ppm with a detection limit of 3.8 nM. As well, the developed method was successfully applied to the detection of Cr6+ in water samples with a satisfactory recovery rate (97.8 %–102.0 %). Analytes in biological media can be rapidly detected and quantified using the assay described herein.

Maryam Nouri: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Leila Hajiaghababaei: Conceptualization, Data curation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Alireza Badiei: Conceptualization, Data curation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Faezeh Khalilian: Writing – review & editing. Ali Mazloomifar: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Islamic Azad University of Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) ShahreRey branch and the University of Tehran for assisting this research financially.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2024.104595.

ce:section.ce:para:
